source: Russian Government estimates; Krivosheev, G.F., 'Soviet Casualties and Combat Losses'
note: Includes ~8.7 million military dead and ~17–18 million civilian deaths. Soviet archives remain partially classified; estimates range from 20–30 million.

source: Krivosheev, G.F. (ed.), 'Soviet Casualties and Combat Losses in the Twentieth Century', 1997
note: Includes combat deaths, non-combat deaths, and POW deaths. Over 5 million Soviet POWs died in German captivity — a deliberate policy of starvation and brutality.

source: Overmans, R., 'Deutsche militärische Verluste im Zweiten Weltkrieg', 1999; German Federal Archives
note: Overmans revised German military dead to 5.318 million; civilian deaths (bombing, expulsion, combat) add 1.5–3 million more. German post-war expellee deaths remain disputed.

source: Chinese government estimates; R.J. Rummel, 'China's Bloody Century'
note: Includes Second Sino-Japanese War (1937–1945). Combines military and civilian deaths from combat, Japanese atrocities (Nanjing), famine, and disease. Estimates vary widely due to limited records.

source: Yad Vashem; US Holocaust Memorial Museum; Hilberg, R., 'The Destruction of the European Jews'
note: Approximately two-thirds of all European Jews were murdered. Largest single-group victim category of the Holocaust. Documented through Yad Vashem's victims database of 4.8 million names.

source: US Holocaust Memorial Museum; Yad Vashem
note: Includes Soviet POWs (~3.3 million), Polish civilians (~1.8 million non-Jews), Roma (~200,000–500,000), disabled persons (~200,000–250,000), Jehovah's Witnesses, gay men, and political prisoners.

source: Institute of National Remembrance (IPN), Poland; Polish Government estimates
note: Approximately 3 million Polish Jews and 2.6 million non-Jewish Poles killed. Poland lost approximately 17% of its pre-war population — the highest percentage of any nation.

source: Japan Defense Agency; Dower, J., 'War Without Mercy'
note: Includes approximately 2.1 million military dead, 500,000–800,000 civilian deaths (including Hiroshima and Nagasaki ~200,000). Excludes Japanese civilian deaths in China and Southeast Asia.

source: UK National Archives; Imperial War Museum
note: Approximately 383,600 military and 67,000 civilian deaths (Blitz and V-weapons). Does not include Commonwealth forces (Australia, Canada, India, South Africa, New Zealand).

source: US Department of Defense; NARA; National WWII Museum
note: Approximately 291,557 in combat; 125,000 non-combat military deaths; minimal civilian deaths. US suffered proportionally far fewer deaths than any other major power.

source: French National Archives; INSEE; Azéma, J-P., 'France at War 1939–1945'
note: Includes ~217,600 military deaths, ~267,000 civilian deaths (including 77,000 French Jews deported to death camps), and deportee deaths. Collaboration-era executions still disputed.

source: Yugoslav government estimates; Tomasevich, J., 'War and Revolution in Yugoslavia'
note: Yugoslavia suffered enormous losses including German and Italian occupation, Croatian Ustasha genocide of Serbs and Jews, and internecine civil war (Chetniks vs. Partisans). Precise breakdown remains contested.

source: Sen, A., 'Poverty and Famines', 1981; Mukerjee, M., 'Churchill's Secret War', 2010
note: Deaths from famine exacerbated by wartime requisitioning, military priorities, and British government policies. Churchill's role contested. India was a British colony; deaths not always included in Allied casualty totals.

category: Hiroshima and Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Deaths
source: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum; Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum; US Strategic Bombing Survey 1946
note: Hiroshima: ~90,000–140,000 deaths by end of 1945; Nagasaki: ~60,000–80,000. Long-term radiation-related cancers added tens of thousands more deaths in subsequent decades.

injured: All survivors experienced serious health effects
source: US Department of Defense; Dower, J., 'War Without Mercy'
note: Of ~140,000 Western Allied POWs held by Japan, approximately 36,000 died — a 27% death rate. Compare to ~4% death rate in German camps for Western Allied POWs. Japanese treatment of POWs was a major war crimes prosecution charge.

source: Italian National Archives; Rochat, G., 'Le guerre italiane 1935–1943'
note: Includes ~301,400 military deaths and 153,000 civilian deaths. Italy suffered heavy losses in the Soviet Union (ARMIR), North Africa, and the Italian campaign after 1943 armistice.

source: Beevor, A., 'Stalingrad'; Glantz, D., 'Armageddon in Stalingrad'
note: The bloodiest single battle in human history. Soviet losses: ~478,000 killed; German/Axis: ~300,000–400,000 killed, wounded, or captured. Of 330,000 Axis troops encircled, only ~91,000 survived to surrender, and only ~6,000 ever returned to Germany.

source: NARA – SHAEF Casualty Reports; Ambrose, S., 'D-Day'
note: Allied casualties on D-Day itself (June 6 only): US ~6,000–9,000, British ~2,700–3,000, Canadian ~1,100. Total Normandy campaign casualties (June–August 1944): ~210,000 Allied, ~240,000 German.

source: NARA – 10th Army records; Feifer, G., 'The Battle of Okinawa'
note: US: ~12,000 killed, ~70,000 wounded. Japanese military: ~110,000 killed. Okinawan civilian: ~100,000–150,000 killed — roughly one-third of the island's pre-war population. The scale of Okinawan civilian deaths directly influenced the decision to use atomic bombs.

source: UNRRA records; Marrus, M., 'The Unwanted: European Refugees in the Twentieth Century'
note: An estimated 40–60 million people were displaced by WWII, including 12–14 million ethnic Germans expelled from Eastern Europe, 6–7 million Jewish survivors seeking new homelands, millions of Soviet forced laborers (Ostarbeiter), and liberated concentration camp survivors.
